html:
Nissha Co., Ltd. (TSE:7915) - ¥1,555

Nissha is a misunderstood Japanese small-mid cap that the market still treats as a tired printing, tablet touch-sensor, and decorative-film supplier. That legacy is real, but it is no longer the whole company. The better frame is a surface-engineering and precision manufacturing business being rebuilt around medical device CDMO, OTC pharmaceutical CDMO, mobility components, and sustainable materials.

The important correction to prior bullish writeups is that this is not a sterile injectable or GLP-1 fill-finish thesis. Nissha’s Medical business is mainly medical device CDMO, own-brand medical products, business media, and OTC pharmaceuticals. That matters because the multiple should not be pure-play injectable CDMO. But it also makes the setup cleaner: investors are not paying for a hot pharma capacity story. They are paying roughly 0.6x book and about 6x current-year EBITDA for a company whose earnings base is shifting toward higher-duration medical and mobility end markets.

The near-term evidence is now visible. In the May 2026 Q1 release, sales were ¥45.8 billion, down 3.5%, but ahead of plan by 12%. Operating profit was only ¥0.75 billion, but the prior forecast was a loss. Medical grew 3.7% with operating profit up 13.3%, while both medical devices CDMO and OTC pharma demand remained solid. Management raised FY2026 guidance to ¥198 billion of sales, ¥7.0 billion of operating profit, and ¥3.2 billion of net income, versus prior guidance of ¥191.5 billion, ¥6.6 billion, and ¥2.3 billion. That is not a huge absolute number, but it is a meaningful change in trajectory.

The market is still anchored to the wrong line item. On reported EPS guidance of ¥67.49, the stock screens optically expensive at 23x earnings. That is the wrong denominator. FY2026 is still absorbing weak H1 tablet demand, acquisition costs, equipment renewal, raw material risk, and corporate drag. The better measure is current-cycle EBITDA: ¥7.0 billion of operating profit plus roughly ¥10 billion of depreciation and amortization puts EBITDA around ¥17 billion. At the current ¥73.7 billion market cap and roughly ¥23 billion of net debt excluding leases, enterprise value is around ¥96 billion, or about 5.5x EBITDA. Including leases, it is still only about 6x.

The hidden torque is in the second-half run-rate. The revised FY2026 plan implies H2 operating profit of ¥5.2 billion on ¥104 billion of sales, or a 5.0% margin. Annualized, that is over ¥10 billion of operating profit before any real payoff from the medical portfolio buildout. Medical alone is guided to ¥56.8 billion of FY2026 sales and ¥4.2 billion of operating profit, but H2 medical margin is still only about 8.4%. If medical reaches a merely good 10% to 12% margin over the next two years, not an heroic 15% to 20%, the consolidated earnings bridge is powerful.

The business transition is deliberate. Nissha has said it wants medical to reach 50% of consolidated sales by 2030. The company integrated Medical Technologies and Pharmaceuticals into one “Medical” segment starting in Q1 because investors found the prior disclosure hard to understand. That is a small but important tell: management knows the market is not seeing the asset clearly. The July 2026 medical device CDMO update should be a relevant catalyst.

The US medical platform is also becoming less abstract. In April, Nissha opened a medical technology R&D facility on Vanderbilt University’s campus in Nashville, intended to connect academic research, clinical application, and medical device development. That fits the actual business: minimally invasive surgical instruments, medical wearable sensors, micro molding, surgical robotics exposure, and development work for large medical device OEMs. This is sticky, qualification-heavy manufacturing, not a commodity print shop.

The new Vietnam acquisition adds optionality, though it should not be oversold. USM Healthcare gives Nissha a Southeast Asia medical device manufacturing base and exposure to stents, orthopedics, and consumables. USM’s 2024 profit was minimal, so the deal is not a near-term earnings fix. But strategically it gives Nissha a low-cost manufacturing and regulatory platform in a fast-growing medical device region. If integrated well, it expands the CDMO footprint beyond the U.S. and Japan.

Industrial Materials is better than the market credits. Decoration demand is being driven by mobility, including exterior functional components for electrification and autonomous driving. Sustainable materials are cyclical, but metallized paper has recovery potential and can still earn acceptable margins. Devices is the messiest segment because tablet touch sensors remain volatile, but Q1 showed management can level production and keep the segment profitable even with weak demand.

Capital allocation is no longer a liability. The stock yields about 3.2% on the planned ¥50 dividend. Book value is roughly ¥2,470 per share, so the stock trades near 0.63x book even after the post-earnings rally. Japan governance pressure, prior buybacks, and management’s explicit focus on portfolio rationalization all matter here. A company trading below tangible asset value while shifting mix toward medical should not be valued like a structurally declining electronics supplier.

The bear case is that this remains a low-margin conglomerate: tablets roll over again, mobility programs disappoint, USM integration drags, and medical margins never clear high single digits. Goodwill and intangibles are large enough that an impairment is possible if the medical plan fails. That is the real risk. But the stock is not priced for a clean execution story. It is priced as if the transition barely works.

Base case: FY2027 to FY2028 EBITDA rises toward ¥20 billion to ¥22 billion as H2 FY2026 margins annualize, medical grows toward ¥60 billion-plus sales, and Industrial Materials normalizes. At 6.5x to 7.0x EBITDA, still a discount to medical CDMO and precision medtech suppliers, equity value lands around ¥2,500 per share.

Bull case: Medical becomes the market’s anchor instead of the legacy tablet business. Medical sales compound, margins move into low double digits, Devices stops being a drag, and consolidated EBITDA reaches ¥25 billion-plus. At 8x EBITDA, reasonable for a medical-led precision manufacturing company with Japanese governance optionality, the stock can reach ¥3,700.

Bear case: earnings stall near FY2025 levels, the market applies a depressed 0.45x to 0.50x book multiple, and the stock falls back to ¥1,150.

At ¥1,555 today, the targets are: bear ¥1,150, down 26%; base ¥2,500, up 61%; bull ¥3,700, up 138%.
